Pegvisomant in acromegaly: a multicenter real-life study in Argentina

evidence_entities.text: Disease control (IGF-1 ≤ 1.2 x ULN) was achieved in 62.9% of patients with a mean dose of 11.8 mg/day
Adverse events related to PEGV were: local injection site reaction in 5.3%, elevated liver enzymes in 9.3%, and tumor size growth in 9.8%
Four out of 50 patients (8%) with available images, showed an increase in tumor size on PEGV
Increased transaminase levels (> 3X ULN) was reported in 7 of 75 (9%) patients

Acromegaly is a chronic disease characterized by excessive secretion of growth hormone (GH), most often from a growth hormone-secreting pituitary adenoma, with resultant hepatic overproduction of insulin-like growth factor I (IGF-١)
GH excess is associated with a significant increase in morbidity, including hypertension, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea, and cancer
